title: Dynamical Control of Superconductivity in Superconductor-Ferromagnet Bilayers
authors: Dimitri Pimenov, Nicholas R. Poniatowski, Charlotte G. L. Bøttcher, Amir Yacoby, Debanjan Chowdhury
abstract: We study a simplified model of a ferromagnetic metal proximitized by a fully-gapped $s-$wave superconductor and integrated with a microwave resonator. The low-energy excitations in the combined system consist of ferromagnetic magnons, Bogoliubov excitations of the superconductor, and cavity photons. We show here that when the magnons and photons have comparable frequencies and are subject to an external drive, the hybridized driven magnon-polaritons induce a non-equilibrium crossover from the expected proximitized nodal $p-$wave superconductor to a fully gapped $(p_x+ip_y)-$superconductor. Moreover, the characteristic crossover temperature is inversely related to the magnon-photon detuning. We compute the temperature-dependent renormalization of the cavity photon frequencies across this nodal to nodeless evolution, which modifies the kinetic inductance of the resonator, and find a number of non-trivial features tied to the non-equilibrium (i.e., driven) nature of the problem. We compare and contrast these results with a recent circuit quantum electrodynamics (cQED) based experiment studying a permalloy-niobium bilayer, where a non-trivial dependence of the low-temperature cavity response on the magnon-photon detuning was observed. Our results pave the way for a principled exploration of engineering novel states of matter by coupling cavity photons to electronic collective modes in correlated two-dimensional materials and interfaces.
summary: The paper theoretically explores controlling superconductivity in superconductor-ferromagnet bilayers by coupling them to an external microwave cavity. By applying a resonant drive, the system undergoes a non-equilibrium phase transition from a nodal p-wave state to a fully gapped superconducting state. This work demonstrates how coherent electromagnetic driving can be used as a tool to engineer exotic quantum phases of matter.
main_problem: The study investigates how coupling superconducting materials (SC) with ferromagnets (FM) to a microwave resonator can dynamically control the superconducting pairing symmetry.
main_result: Driven magnon-polaritons induce a non-equilibrium crossover from an expected nodal p-wave superconductor state to a fully gapped $(p_x+ip_y)$-superconductor.
method: The analysis uses theoretical modeling involving coupled Bogoliubov excitations, ferromagnetic magnons, and cavity photons, often employing perturbation theory and time-averaging techniques.
model_or_system: The system is an SC-FM bilayer integrated with a microwave resonator, modeled by coupling the superconducting gap dynamics to the magnon-photon polariton modes. The low-energy excitations include magnons ($\omega_m$), Bogoliubov quasiparticles, and cavity photons ($\omega_r$).
key_observables: The temperature-dependent shift of the cavity photon frequency, $\delta\omega_r(T)$, is used to diagnose the superconducting gap symmetry (nodal vs. nodeless).
important_parameters: Magnon-photon detuning ($\Delta$), external drive strength ($V_d$), and temperature ($T$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes weak, slow driving fields and utilizes approximations like integrating out certain degrees of freedom or using the adiabatic limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the coupled system setup (SC-FM/resonator), magnon-polariton dispersion, and theoretical predictions for $\delta\omega_r(T)$ under both equilibrium and driven conditions, highlighting characteristic power laws.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the coupled Hamiltonian, then analyzing the dynamics of the polaritons under external driving, which in turn dictates the non-equilibrium evolution of the superconducting pairing state ($\Delta_k$), culminating in predictions for measurable spectroscopic signatures like $\delta\omega_r(T)$.
why_interesting: This work sits at the intersection of circuit QED and condensed matter theory, providing a framework to engineer novel quantum states (like changing gap symmetry) using coherent electromagnetic driving fields.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The normal (t) and spin- orbit assisted (tsoc) tunneling induces triplet superconduct- ivity in the FM layer; see Eq. (4). (b) Left: Within linear- response, temperature-dependent shift of the resonator fre- quency δωr(T) probes the equilibrium nodal-gap ∆k of the superconductor. Right: Driving renders ∆(t) time-dependent and nodeless, modifying δωr(T). Spintronics.note: The core mechanism described—coupling superconducting dynamics to magnon-photon polaritons in an SC-FM bilayer driven by a cavity resonator—directly matches the theme of magnons/spin waves coupled to cavity photons and non-equilibrium phases in hybrid spin-photon systems.
